# Data Exploration

## Loading the Data

First, I'll load the necessary packages and read in the data:

# Load the fpp3 package suite for forecasting
library(fpp3)



# Read the CSV file and convert to a time series object (tsibble)
baregg <- read.csv("BareggTunnel.csv") |>
  mutate(Day = dmy(Day)) |>  # Parse the date column
  as_tsibble(index = Day)     # Tell R this is time series data

# Look at the first few rows
head(baregg)

# Get basic statistics
summary(baregg$Number.of.vehicles)


## Visualizing the Time Series

autoplot(baregg, Number.of.vehicles) +
  labs(title = "Baregg Tunnel Daily Traffic (Nov 2003 - Nov 2005)",
       y = "Number of Vehicles", 
       x = "Date") +
  theme_minimal()


baregg |>
  gg_subseries(Number.of.vehicles, period = "week") +
  labs(title = "Traffic Patterns by Day of Week",
       y = "Number of Vehicles") +
  theme_minimal()



## The Two Models I'm Comparing

### Model 1: Naïve Forecast

The Naïve method is the simplest possible forecast. It just says "tomorrow will be like today." More formally:

$$\hat{y}_{t+h} = y_t$$


### Model 2: Linear Regression (TSLM)

This model tries to capture the patterns I observed:

$$y_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \times \text{trend} + \sum \beta_i \times \text{DayOfWeek}_i + \varepsilon_t$$


## Splitting the Data

# Split into training and validation sets
train <- baregg |>
  filter(Day < ymd("2005-07-01"))

valid <- baregg |>
  filter(Day >= ymd("2005-07-01"))

cat("Training observations:", nrow(train), "\n")
cat("Validation observations:", nrow(valid), "\n")


# Fitting the Models

## Step 3: Train Both Models

### Naïve Model


# Fit the Naïve forecast on training data
fit_naive <- train |>
  model(Naive = NAIVE(Number.of.vehicles))

fit_naive


### Linear Regression Model


# Fit linear regression with trend and weekly seasonality
fit_lm <- train |>
  model(
    LinReg = TSLM(Number.of.vehicles ~ 
                    trend() + season("week"))
  )

# Look at the coefficients
report(fit_lm)


### Generate Forecasts

# Generate forecasts for the validation period
fc_naive <- fit_naive |>
  forecast(new_data = valid)

fc_lm <- fit_lm |>
  forecast(new_data = valid)


fc_naive |>
  autoplot(baregg, level = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Naïve Forecast vs Actual Traffic",
       subtitle = "Validation Period: July - November 2005",
       y = "Number of Vehicles", 
       x = "Date") +
  theme_minimal()




### Visualize the Linear Regression Forecast

fc_lm |>
  autoplot(baregg, level = NULL) +
  labs(title = "Linear Regression Forecast vs Actual Traffic",
       subtitle = "Validation Period: July - November 2005",
       y = "Number of Vehicles", 
       x = "Date") +
  theme_minimal()


### Side-by-Side Comparison

# Combine forecasts for comparison
both_forecasts <- bind_rows(
  fc_naive |> mutate(Model = "Naïve"),
  fc_lm |> mutate(Model = "Linear Regression")
)

# Plot both
both_forecasts |>
  autoplot(baregg, level = NULL) +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, ncol = 1) +
  labs(title = "Comparing Both Forecast Methods",
       y = "Number of Vehicles", 
       x = "Date") +
  theme_minimal()

### Calculate Accuracy Metrics


# Get accuracy metrics for both models
acc_naive <- accuracy(fc_naive, baregg)
acc_lm <- accuracy(fc_lm, baregg)

# Combine into one table for comparison
accuracy_table <- bind_rows(
  acc_naive |> mutate(Model = "Naïve"),
  acc_lm |> mutate(Model = "Linear Regression")
) |>
  select(Model, ME, RMSE, MAE, MAPE, MASE, ACF1)

accuracy_table

# Calculate the scaling factor (MAE of Naïve forecast on training data)
mae_naive_train <- mean(abs(diff(train$Number.of.vehicles)))

# Calculate MASE for both models
mase_naive <- mean(abs(valid$Number.of.vehicles - fc_naive$.mean)) / mae_naive_train
mase_linreg <- mean(abs(valid$Number.of.vehicles - fc_lm$.mean)) / mae_naive_train

cat("Manual MASE Calculation:\n")
cat("------------------------\n")
cat("MASE for Naïve:     ", round(mase_naive, 3), "\n")
cat("MASE for Lin Reg:   ", round(mase_linreg, 3), "\n")
cat("\nInterpretation:\n")
cat("MASE < 1 means the forecast is better than the Naïve benchmark\n")
cat("MASE = 1 means it's equal to Naïve\n")
cat("MASE > 1 means it's worse than Naïve\n")


## Step 5: Diagnosing Forecast Errors

Now I need to check if my forecast errors look random or if there are patterns I'm missing.

### Diagnostic Plots for Naïve Model

# Three diagnostic plots in one
fit_naive |>
  gg_tsresiduals() +
  labs(title = "Naïve Model: Residual Diagnostics")

### Diagnostic Plots for Linear Regression


fit_lm |>
  gg_tsresiduals() +
  labs(title = "Linear Regression Model: Residual Diagnostics")

This suggests the Linear Regression model is capturing most of the systematic patterns.

### Looking at Forecast Errors Over Time

# Calculate errors for the validation period
errors_naive <- valid$Number.of.vehicles - fc_naive$.mean
errors_lm <- valid$Number.of.vehicles - fc_lm$.mean

# Create a dataframe for plotting
error_df <- tibble(
  Day = valid$Day,
  Naive_Error = errors_naive,
  LinReg_Error = errors_lm
)

# Plot errors over time
error_df |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Naive_Error, LinReg_Error), 
               names_to = "Model", values_to = "Error") |>
  mutate(Model = recode(Model, 
                        "Naive_Error" = "Naïve",
                        "LinReg_Error" = "Linear Regression")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Day, y = Error, color = Model)) +
  geom_line() +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Forecast Errors Over Time",
       y = "Error (Actual - Forecast)",
       x = "Date") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

### Error Distributions

error_df |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(Naive_Error, LinReg_Error), 
               names_to = "Model", values_to = "Error") |>
  mutate(Model = recode(Model, 
                        "Naive_Error" = "Naïve",
                        "LinReg_Error" = "Linear Regression")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = Error, fill = Model)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 20, alpha = 0.7) +
  facet_wrap(~ Model, ncol = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Forecast Errors",
       x = "Error",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

par(mfrow = c(2, 1))
acf(errors_naive, main = "ACF of Naïve Model Errors", lag.max = 20)
acf(errors_lm, main = "ACF of Linear Regression Errors", lag.max = 20)
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))

cat("Mean Error Analysis:\n")
cat("--------------------\n")
cat("Naïve ME:          ", round(acc_naive$ME, 2), "\n")
cat("Linear Regression ME:", round(acc_lm$ME, 2), "\n\n")

cat("Interpretation:\n")
cat("• ME close to 0 = unbiased (errors average out)\n")
cat("• ME > 0 = over-forecasting on average\n")
cat("• ME < 0 = under-forecasting on average\n")

cat("Error Distribution Statistics:\n")
cat("-------------------------------\n")
cat("Naïve errors:\n")
cat("  Mean:   ", round(mean(errors_naive), 2), "\n")
cat("  Median: ", round(median(errors_naive), 2), "\n\n")

cat("Linear Regression errors:\n")
cat("  Mean:   ", round(mean(errors_lm), 2), "\n")
cat("  Median: ", round(median(errors_lm), 2), "\n")
